books.title: The Red and the Black
books.author: Stendhal
books.review: Best translation is the one by Robert M. Adams. Burton Raffel translation is good too.

books.title: The Rest is Noise
books.author: Ross
books.review: Runs through the personalities of the 20th century. My favorite book on music.

books.title: Simple Rules for a Complex World
books.author: Epstein

books.title: Swann's Way
books.author: Proust
books.review: Translation by Lydia Davis

books.title: Zero to One
books.author: Thiel & Masters
books.review: Based on CS 183 lecture notes. If you must pick one, read the lecture notes. The radicalism of the ideas were mainstreamed for the book.

books.title: The Idea Factory
books.author: Gertner
books.review: History of the place that labs that developed radar, transistors, satellites, cell phone telephony, and more.

books.title: Worldly Philosopher: The Odyssey of Albert O. Hirschman
books.author: Adelman

books.title: The Rational Optimist
books.author: Ridley
books.review: A wonderful book about the scientific achievements that's given us better nutrition, longer lifespans, and easier access to energy, etc.

books.title: Dialogues (Crito and Phaedo)
books.author: Plato
books.review: In particular Crito and Phaedo.

books.title: Effi Briest
books.author: Fontane
books.review: Translation by Ritchie Robertson. There's so much nuance; the most exciting plot detail isn't even described, only discussed afterwards.

books.title: Radicals for Capitalism
books.author: Doherty
books.review: Details the rise of the American libertarian movement. Serious study, but the personalities are so crazy that it's fun to read.

books.title: The Economic Naturalist
books.author: Frank
books.review: Short stories about the economic way of thinking.

books.title: The World as I Found It
books.author: Duffy
books.review: A novel about the interactions of Russell, Wittgenstein, and G.E. Moore. Very fun.

books.title: The Undercover Economist
books.author: Harford
books.review: Best parts were the sections about price discrimination.

books.title: The Myth of the Rational Voter
books.author: Caplan

books.title: The Righteous Mind
books.author: Haidt
books.review: Kevin Simler put it best: "How humans actually, empirically, think about morality."

books.title: Bourgeois Virtues & Bourgeois Dignity
books.author: McCloskey
books.review: First 50 pages of the Apology in Virtues is most worth reading.

books.title: The Moon is a Harsh Mistress
books.author: Heinlein

books.title: Min Kamp Vol. 1
books.author: Knausgaard
books.review: Uncomfortable, mesmerizing.

books.title: The Worldly Philosophers
books.author: Heilbroner
books.review: The first economics-y book I read; I spent two years of undergrad wondering when we were going to cover Thorstein Veblen.

books.title: Nudge
books.author: Thaler & Sunstein
books.review: Super well argued, I couldn't continue to be a knee-jerk skeptic.

books.title: The Color of Magic
books.author: Terry Pratchett
books.review: When I was little I read most of the Discworld series. I really liked them, but haven't picked any of them up again.

books.title: Cryptonomicon
books.author: Stephenson
books.review: A lot of science wrapped in a thrilling story.

books.title: Humanity: A Moral History of the Twentieth Century
books.author: Glover

books.title: Boomerang
books.author: Lewis
books.review: More fun than The Big Short, not quite as good as Liar's Poker.

books.title: The Threepenny Opera
books.author: Brecht
books.review: Translated by Ralph Manheim, try to go see it in original German.

books.title: How the Scots Invented the Modern World
books.author: Herman

books.title: Duino Elegies
books.author: Rilke
books.review: Translation by Stephen Mitchell

books.title: Memory Chalet
books.author: Judt
books.review: Excellent essays on growing up in '50s/'60s Europe.

books.title: The Magic Mountain
books.author: Mann
books.review: Translation by J.E. Woods. First 150 pages or so are boring, but it picks up. The ruminations on death/dying make it worth it.

books.title: The Diamond Age
books.author: Stephenson
books.review: Far better I feel than Snow Crash

books.title: Ninety Percent of Everything: Inside Shipping
books.author: George
books.review: I love ships and ships love me back. This is pretty much the perfect nonfiction book: many, many interesting facts weaved into a narrative story; and it's slim, and doesn't go overboard with too much detail.

books.title: The Wonderful Future that Never Was
books.author: Benford

books.title: The Box
books.author: Marc Levinson
books.review: I now work at a company that arranges for air and ocean freight

books.title: The Man in the High Castle
books.author: PKD

books.title: King Larry: The Life and Ruins of a Billionaire Genius
books.author: Scurlock

books.title: The German War: A Nation Under Arms, 1939-1945
books.author: Stargardt
books.review: The war from the perspective of the German population. One interesting fact: A quarter of Goebbel's budget was spent on theatre, which was about as much as he spent on propaganda, and more than twice as much on film.

books.title: Factory Girls: From Village to City in a Changing China
books.author: Chang

books.title: The Queen's Throat
books.author: Koestenbaum
books.review: A remarkable book; this is how you write about opera.

books.title: The Tang Dynasty
books.author: Lewis
books.review: What's important about Tang? Drainage projects in the south (Jiangnan and Lingnan), making it the permanent economic center of the empire; institutionalization of the Sui Codes; breaking of the aristocratic families.

books.title: The Second World Wars
books.author: Hanson
books.review: At some point one should decide to stop reading about this topic. This superb book is about it for me.

books.title: Slow Days, Fast Company: The World, The Flesh, and L.A.: Tales
books.author: Babitz

books.title: King Lear
books.author: Shakespeare
books.review: The heaps of suffering make it feel a little bit ridiculous by the end. Otherwise, it is very good.

books.title: Some Remarks: Essays and Other Writing
books.author: Stephenson
books.review: Worth reading for the Stephenson fan. And which geek wouldn't be?

books.title: We Were Burning: Japanese Entrepreneurs And The Forging Of The Electronic Age
books.author: Johnstone
books.review: The book does not live up to its fantastic title. Heaps of facts, but not really conceptually-driven, and I did not find that I was able to draw many broader lessons.

books.title: The Age of Confucian Rule: The Song Transformation of China
books.author: Kuhn

books.title: The Troubled Empire: China in the Yuan and Ming Dynasties
books.author: Brook
books.review: The best in the HUP History of Imperial China series; most analytically-driven, a focus on the right topics, and most cleverly written. It's helpful to learn the impact of the Little Ice Age on the Ming.

books.title: The Hall of Uselessness: Collected Essays
books.author: Leys
books.review: Very good reflections on Chinese aesthetics, with a focus on painting and calligraphy

books.title: China's Last Empire: The Great Qing
books.author: Rowe

books.title: Exact Thinking in Demented Times
books.author: Sigmund
books.review: An excellent book about some extraordinary people. It's as demented as advertised.

books.title: The Water Kingdom: A Secret History of China
books.author: Ball
books.review: I thought this was quite bad, so I'm encouraging you to skip it

books.title: The Power to Compete
books.author: Mikitani and Mikitani
books.review: My favorite genre of book: A realization that economic growth has been way too slow, and constructive proposals to accelerate it

books.title: The German Economy During the Nineteenth Century
books.author: Pierenkemper and Tilly

books.title: A Great Leap Forward: 1930s Depression and U.S. Economic Growth

books.title: Iron Kingdom: The Rise and Downfall of Prussia, 1600-1947

books.title: The Story of a New Name
books.author: Ferrante

books.title: Those Who Leave and Those Who Stay
books.author: Ferrante

books.title: Dunford
